sources.notes: Statement and constraints: grid cells, needy cells, ordinary/special values, target value at least two thirds, and no special cell adjacent to a needy cell.
Used to confirm the three-color grid construction, cycle breaking by deleting ordinary cells, and DFS output order.

reference_candidates.notes: Candidate Luogu solution page; not used as evidence in this review.

annotation.summary: Choose and order grid cells to blacken so all needy cells are chosen, each chosen cell has at most one earlier black neighbor, and the chosen non-needy value is at least two thirds of the total.
annotation.constraints: n*m <= 2000, sum (n*m)^2 <= 4e6, a <= b, and special cells are not adjacent to needy cells.
annotation.core_idea: A legal move order follows from any forest in the chosen grid graph: DFS preorder gives every vertex at most one earlier chosen neighbor. For each residue class of (i+j) mod 3, initially keep the other two classes plus all needy cells; by pigeonhole one choice keeps at least 2P/3 non-needy value. Adding needy cells can create cycles, so repeatedly detect a cycle and delete an ordinary non-special cell on it. The no-special-adjacent-to-needy condition lets these deletions be charged safely while preserving the value bound. The remaining induced graph is a forest, so output a DFS order.
annotation.complexity: O((n*m)^2) per test in the official-style implementation.
tricks: three-color the grid and keep two residue classes
interpret the move order as constructing an acyclic induced subgraph
delete ordinary cells from cycles while preserving the value bound

solution_variants.name: three-color-forest-cycle-pruning
solution_variants.summary: Try each skipped residue class modulo 3; include the other residues and all needy cells, delete ordinary cells until the selected subgraph is acyclic, then output DFS preorder.

tags.evidence: The task asks for an explicit legal move sequence, and the solution constructs a high-value forest plus its DFS order.
Among the three residues of (i+j) mod 3, keeping the other two guarantees at least two thirds of the total non-needy value for some choice.
The proof that cycles can be repaired by deleting ordinary cells relies on the embedded grid/planar structure around inserted needy cells.
tags.description: Problems whose key proof or construction relies on planar graph structure, such as faces, embedded cycles, or Euler-style charging, beyond generic graph traversal.
tags.created_reason: Existing graph tags cover DFS and cycles, but not planar-embedding arguments used in constructive grid/graph proofs.

tags.evidence: DFS is used to detect cycles in the selected grid subgraph and to output a preorder satisfying the move rule.
The algorithm repeatedly removes a vertex from a detected cycle until the selected induced subgraph is a forest.
